title: Quick Start
text: Get up and running with kmeanssa-ng in 5 minutes! This guide will walk you through your first clustering example.

title: Quick Start
text:
Basic Example
Let’s cluster points on a simple quantum graph with two natural clusters:

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, QGSimulatedAnnealing

# Step 1: Create a quantum graph with block structure
graph = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[30, 30],  # Two clusters of 30 nodes each
    p=[
        [0.8, 0.1],  # 80% intra-cluster edges, 10% inter-cluster
        [0.1, 0.8],
    ],  # 10% inter-cluster, 80% intra-cluster
)

# Step 2: Precompute distances (important for performance!)
graph.precomputing()

# Step 3: Sample random points on the graph
points = graph.sample_points(100)  # 100 points distributed on edges

print(graph)
print(f"Created graph with {graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes and {graph.number_of_edges()} edges")
print(f"Sampled {len(points)} points on the graph")

QuantumGraph named 'stochastic_block_model' with 60 nodes and 783 edges
Created graph with 60 nodes and 783 edges
Sampled 100 points on the graph



# Step 4: Run k-means clustering
sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(
    observations=points,  # Correct parameter name
    k=2,  # Number of clusters
    lambda_param=1,  # Temperature parameter
    beta=1.0,  # Drift strength
    step_size=0.1,  # Step size for random walks
)

# Step 5: Get cluster centers
centers = sa.run(
    robust_prop=0.1,  # Use last 10% iterations for averaging
    initialization="kpp",  # k-means++ initialization
    algorithm_version="v1",  # Algorithm variant
)

print(f"Found {len(centers)} cluster centers")
print(f"Centers located at: {[str(c) for c in centers]}")

Found 2 cluster centers
Centers located at: ["QGPoint on 'stochastic_block_model' edge (4, 24) at position 0.000", "QGPoint on 'stochastic_block_model' edge (39, 58) at position 0.000"]

title: Quick Start
text:
Understanding the Results
The algorithm returns centers - a list of QGCenter objects representing the optimal cluster centers. Each center has:

edge: The edge where the center is located
position: Position on the edge (between 0 and 1)


for i, center in enumerate(centers):
    print(f"Cluster {i+1}: {center}")

Cluster 1: QGPoint on 'stochastic_block_model' edge (4, 24) at position 0.000
Cluster 2: QGPoint on 'stochastic_block_model' edge (39, 58) at position 0.000

Customizing the Algorithm

Algorithm Parameters

# More exploration (higher temperature)
sa_explore = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2, lambda_param=2, beta=0.5)

# More exploitation (stronger drift)
sa_exploit = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2, lambda_param=0.5, beta=2.0)

# Smaller steps (more precise)
sa_precise = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2, step_size=0.05)

print("Parameter variations created successfully")

Parameter variations created successfully




Different Graph Types

Stochastic Block Model

# Custom block probabilities
graph_3clusters = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[20, 30, 25], 
    p=[[0.9, 0.1, 0.05],
       [0.1, 0.8, 0.15], 
       [0.05, 0.15, 0.85]]
)
graph_3clusters.precomputing()
print(f"3-cluster graph: {graph_3clusters.number_of_nodes()} nodes")

3-cluster graph: 75 nodes




Simple Test Graph

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_simple_graph

# Symmetric two-cluster graph
simple_graph = generate_simple_graph(
    n_a=10,          # Nodes in cluster A
    n_aa=5,          # Extra connections in cluster A  
    bridge_length=3.0 # Distance between clusters
)
simple_graph.precomputing()
print(f"Simple graph: {simple_graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes")

Simple graph: 122 nodes




From NetworkX

import networkx as nx
from kmeanssa_ng import as_quantum_graph

# Use any NetworkX graph
G = nx.karate_club_graph()
quantum_karate = as_quantum_graph(G, edge_length=1.0)
quantum_karate.precomputing()
print(f"Karate club as quantum graph: {quantum_karate.number_of_nodes()} nodes")

Performance Tips
Speed Up Your Clustering:

Always call precomputing() - This caches shortest paths
Use appropriate robust_prop - 0.1 (10%) is usually sufficient

Start with k-means++ - Set initialization="kpp"
Tune parameters gradually - Start with defaults, then adjust


# Fast setup for large graphs

sa_fast = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points, k=3, step_size=0.2)  # Larger steps
centers_fast = sa_fast.run(robust_prop=0.05, initialization="kpp")  # Less robustification
print(centers_fast)
print(f"Fast clustering found {len(centers_fast)} centers")

title: Custom Metric Spaces
text: While quantum graphs are powerful, you can extend kmeanssa-ng to work with any metric space by implementing the abstract base classes. This guide shows you how to create custom metric spaces for specialized clustering applications.

title: Custom Metric Spaces
text:
Understanding the Architecture
The library uses a three-layer abstraction pattern:

Point - Immutable elements in your metric space
Center - Movable points that serve as cluster centers

Space - The metric space containing points and distance computation


from kmeanssa_ng.core.abstract import Point, Center, Space
from kmeanssa_ng import SimulatedAnnealing
import numpy as np
import math

print("Abstract base classes imported successfully!")

Abstract base classes imported successfully!

title: Custom Metric Spaces
text:
Creating a Simple 1D Metric Space
Let’s start with a basic example - clustering on a line (1D space):

# 1D Point implementation
class LinePoint(Point):
    def __init__(self, space, x: float):
        self._space = space
        self.x = x
    
    @property 
    def space(self):
        return self._space
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"LinePoint(x={self.x:.2f})"
    
    def __eq__(self, other):
        return isinstance(other, LinePoint) and abs(self.x - other.x) < 1e-10
    
    def __hash__(self):
        return hash(round(self.x, 10))

# 1D Center implementation  
class LineCenter(LinePoint, Center):
    def __init__(self, space, x: float):
        super().__init__(space, x)
    
    def brownian_motion(self, time_to_travel: float):
        """Random walk along the line."""
        step = np.random.normal(0, math.sqrt(time_to_travel))
        self.x += step
        
        # Keep within space bounds
        self.x = max(self._space.min_x, min(self._space.max_x, self.x))
    
    def drift(self, target_point: Point, prop_to_travel: float):
        """Move toward target point along the line."""
        if not isinstance(target_point, LinePoint):
            raise ValueError("Target must be a LinePoint")
        
        direction = target_point.x - self.x
        self.x += direction * prop_to_travel
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"LineCenter(x={self.x:.2f})"

# 1D Space implementation
class LineSpace(Space):
    def __init__(self, min_x: float = 0.0, max_x: float = 10.0):
        self.min_x = min_x
        self.max_x = max_x
    
    def distance(self, p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float:
        """Euclidean distance on the line."""
        if not isinstance(p1, LinePoint) or not isinstance(p2, LinePoint):
            raise ValueError("Points must be LinePoint instances")
        return abs(p1.x - p2.x)
    
    def sample_points(self, n: int) -> list[Point]:
        """Sample random points uniformly on the line."""
        points = []
        for _ in range(n):
            x = np.random.uniform(self.min_x, self.max_x)
            points.append(LinePoint(self, x))
        return points
    
    def sample_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Sample random centers uniformly on the line."""
        centers = []
        for _ in range(k):
            x = np.random.uniform(self.min_x, self.max_x)
            centers.append(LineCenter(self, x))
        return centers
    
    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """k-means++ initialization for 1D space."""
        return self.sample_centers(k)  # Simple fallback for 1D
    
    def compute_clusters(self, centers: list[Center]):
        """Not needed for basic usage."""
        pass
    
    def calculate_energy_graph(self, centers: list[Center]) -> float:
        """Calculate clustering energy (not used in this example)."""
        return 0.0
    
    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers."""
        return [self.distance(point, center) for center in centers]

# Test the 1D space
line_space = LineSpace(0, 10)
points_1d = line_space.sample_points(20)
centers_1d = line_space.sample_centers(3)

print(f"Sample 1D points: {points_1d[:5]}")
print(f"Sample 1D centers: {centers_1d}")
print(f"Distance between first two points: {line_space.distance(points_1d[0], points_1d[1]):.2f}")

Sample 1D points: [LinePoint(x=9.00), LinePoint(x=5.02), LinePoint(x=4.83), LinePoint(x=9.98), LinePoint(x=3.65)]
Sample 1D centers: [LineCenter(x=7.13), LineCenter(x=7.04), LineCenter(x=8.90)]
Distance between first two points: 3.97



Clustering on the Line

# Run simulated annealing on 1D space
sa_1d = SimulatedAnnealing(points_1d, k=3)
final_centers = sa_1d.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)

print(f"Final centers: {final_centers}")

# Calculate final energy
energy_1d = sa_1d.calculate_energy(final_centers, points_1d)
print(f"Final clustering energy: {energy_1d:.3f}")

# Show cluster assignments
print("\nCluster assignments:")
for i, point in enumerate(points_1d):
    distances = [line_space.distance(point, center) for center in final_centers]
    closest_cluster = np.argmin(distances)
    print(f"Point {i}: x={point.x:.2f} → Cluster {closest_cluster}")

title: Custom Metric Spaces
Custom Metric Spaces
text:
Specialized Example: Circular Space
Here’s an example of clustering on a circle, where distances wrap around:

# Circular space (angles on a circle)
class CircularPoint(Point):
    def __init__(self, space, angle: float):
        self._space = space  
        self.angle = angle % (2 * math.pi)  # Normalize to [0, 2π)
    
    @property
    def space(self):
        return self._space
    
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"CircularPoint(θ={self.angle:.2f})"

class CircularCenter(CircularPoint, Center):
    def brownian_motion(self, time_to_travel: float):
        """Random walk on the circle."""
        step = np.random.normal(0, math.sqrt(time_to_travel))
        self.angle = (self.angle + step) % (2 * math.pi)
    
    def drift(self, target_point: Point, prop_to_travel: float):
        """Move toward target on circle (shortest path)."""
        if not isinstance(target_point, CircularPoint):
            raise ValueError("Target must be CircularPoint")
        
        # Calculate shortest angular distance
        diff = target_point.angle - self.angle
        
        # Wrap to [-π, π] for shortest path
        while diff > math.pi:
            diff -= 2 * math.pi
        while diff < -math.pi:
            diff += 2 * math.pi
        
        self.angle = (self.angle + diff * prop_to_travel) % (2 * math.pi)

class CircularSpace(Space):
    def distance(self, p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float:
        """Angular distance on circle."""
        if not isinstance(p1, CircularPoint) or not isinstance(p2, CircularPoint):
            raise ValueError("Points must be CircularPoint instances")
        
        diff = abs(p1.angle - p2.angle)
        return min(diff, 2 * math.pi - diff)  # Shortest arc
    
    def sample_points(self, n: int) -> list[Point]:
        """Sample random angles."""
        points = []
        for _ in range(n):
            angle = np.random.uniform(0, 2 * math.pi)
            points.append(CircularPoint(self, angle))
        return points
    
    def sample_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Sample random center angles."""
        centers = []
        for _ in range(k):
            angle = np.random.uniform(0, 2 * math.pi)
            centers.append(CircularCenter(self, angle))
        return centers
    
    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """k-means++ initialization for circular space."""
        return self.sample_centers(k)  # Simple fallback
    
    def compute_clusters(self, centers: list[Center]):
        pass
    
    def calculate_energy_graph(self, centers: list[Center]) -> float:
        return 0.0
    
    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers."""
        return [self.distance(point, center) for center in centers]

# Test circular clustering
circular_space = CircularSpace()

# Create clustered data (3 groups around the circle)
clustered_points = []
cluster_centers = [0.5, 2.5, 4.5]  # Roughly evenly spaced

for center in cluster_centers:
    for _ in range(15):
        # Add some noise around each cluster center
        noise = np.random.normal(0, 0.3)
        angle = center + noise
        clustered_points.append(CircularPoint(circular_space, angle))

print(f"Created {len(clustered_points)} points in 3 circular clusters")

# Run clustering
sa_circular = SimulatedAnnealing(clustered_points, k=3)
circular_centers = sa_circular.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
circular_energy = sa_circular.calculate_energy(circular_centers, clustered_points)

print(f"Circular clustering energy: {circular_energy:.3f}")
print(f"Found centers at angles: {[f'{c.angle:.2f}' for c in circular_centers]}")

# Verify clustering makes sense
for i, center in enumerate(circular_centers):
    nearby_points = [p for p in clustered_points if circular_space.distance(p, center) < 1.0]
    print(f"Cluster {i}: {len(nearby_points)} points near angle {center.angle:.2f}")

Created 45 points in 3 circular clusters
Circular clustering energy: 1.213
Found centers at angles: ['2.47', '3.25', '4.25']
Cluster 0: 15 points near angle 2.47
Cluster 1: 14 points near angle 3.25
Cluster 2: 15 points near angle 4.25

title: Custom Metric Spaces
text:
Integration with k-means++ Initialization
For optimal results, implement k-means++ initialization in your Space class:

# Enhanced 2D space with proper k-means++
class Enhanced2DSpace(Space2D):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self._cached_points = None
    
    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Proper k-means++ initialization."""
        # Generate a reference set of points for initialization
        if self._cached_points is None:
            self._cached_points = self.sample_points(200)  # Cache for efficiency
        
        if k <= 0:
            return []
        
        centers = []
        
        # Choose first center randomly
        first_point = np.random.choice(self._cached_points)
        centers.append(Center2D(self, first_point.x, first_point.y))
        
        # Choose remaining centers with probability proportional to squared distance
        for _ in range(k - 1):
            distances = []
            
            for point in self._cached_points:
                min_dist = min(self.distance(point, center) for center in centers)
                distances.append(min_dist * min_dist)  # Squared distance
            
            # Weighted random selection
            total = sum(distances)
            if total > 0:
                probs = [d / total for d in distances]
                chosen_idx = np.random.choice(len(self._cached_points), p=probs)
                chosen_point = self._cached_points[chosen_idx]
                centers.append(Center2D(self, chosen_point.x, chosen_point.y))
        
        return centers

# Test enhanced initialization
enhanced_space = Enhanced2DSpace(0, 10, 0, 10, "euclidean")
points_enhanced = enhanced_space.sample_points(100)

# Compare random vs k-means++ initialization
sa_random = SimulatedAnnealing(points_enhanced, k=4)
centers_random = sa_random.run(initialization="random", robust_prop=0.05)
energy_random = sa_random.calculate_energy(centers_random, points_enhanced)

sa_kpp = SimulatedAnnealing(points_enhanced, k=4)
centers_kpp = sa_kpp.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.05)  
energy_kpp = sa_kpp.calculate_energy(centers_kpp, points_enhanced)

print(f"Random initialization energy: {energy_random:.3f}")
print(f"k-means++ initialization energy: {energy_kpp:.3f}")
print(f"Improvement: {energy_random - energy_kpp:.3f}")

Random initialization energy: 9.931
k-means++ initialization energy: 6.918
Improvement: 3.013

title: Custom Metric Spaces
text:
Advanced Features and Optimizations

Batch Distance Computation
For performance, implement batch distance operations:

class OptimizedSpace2D(Enhanced2DSpace):
    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers efficiently."""
        if not isinstance(point, Point2D):
            raise ValueError("Point must be Point2D")
        
        distances = []
        for center in centers:
            if isinstance(center, Point2D):
                distances.append(self.distance(point, center))
            else:
                distances.append(float('inf'))
        
        return distances

# Test batch computation
opt_space = OptimizedSpace2D()
test_centers = opt_space.sample_centers(5)
test_point = Point2D(opt_space, 5.0, 5.0)

batch_distances = opt_space.batch_distances_from_centers(test_centers, test_point)
individual_distances = [opt_space.distance(test_point, c) for c in test_centers]

print(f"Batch distances: {[f'{d:.2f}' for d in batch_distances]}")
print(f"Individual distances: {[f'{d:.2f}' for d in individual_distances]}")
print(f"Results match: {np.allclose(batch_distances, individual_distances)}")

Batch distances: ['1.96', '3.10', '6.41', '0.46', '5.54']
Individual distances: ['1.96', '3.10', '6.41', '0.46', '5.54']
Results match: True




Custom Similarity Spaces
Create spaces based on similarity rather than distance:

# Similarity-based space (higher values = more similar)
class SimilaritySpace(Space):
    def __init__(self, similarity_matrix: np.ndarray, labels: list = None):
        self.similarity_matrix = similarity_matrix
        self.n_objects = similarity_matrix.shape[0]
        self.labels = labels or list(range(self.n_objects))
        
        # Convert similarities to distances (distance = max_sim - similarity)
        self.max_sim = np.max(similarity_matrix)
        self.distance_matrix = self.max_sim - similarity_matrix
    
    def distance(self, p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float:
        """Distance derived from similarity."""
        if not isinstance(p1, SimilarityPoint) or not isinstance(p2, SimilarityPoint):
            raise ValueError("Points must be SimilarityPoint instances")
        return self.distance_matrix[p1.object_id, p2.object_id]
    
    def sample_points(self, n: int) -> list[Point]:
        """Sample object IDs."""
        points = []
        for _ in range(n):
            obj_id = np.random.randint(0, self.n_objects)
            points.append(SimilarityPoint(self, obj_id))
        return points
    
    def sample_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """Sample object IDs as centers."""
        centers = []
        chosen_ids = np.random.choice(self.n_objects, size=min(k, self.n_objects), replace=False)
        for obj_id in chosen_ids:
            centers.append(SimilarityCenter(self, obj_id))
        return centers
    
    def sample_kpp_centers(self, k: int) -> list[Center]:
        """k-means++ initialization for similarity space."""
        return self.sample_centers(k)  # Simple fallback
    
    def compute_clusters(self, centers: list[Center]):
        pass
    
    def calculate_energy_graph(self, centers: list[Center]) -> float:
        return 0.0
    
    def batch_distances_from_centers(self, centers: list[Center], point: Point) -> list[float]:
        """Compute distances from a point to all centers."""
        return [self.distance(point, center) for center in centers]

class SimilarityPoint(Point):
    def __init__(self, space, object_id: int):
        self._space = space
        self.object_id = object_id
    
    @property
    def space(self):
        return self._space
    
    def __repr__(self):
        label = self._space.labels[self.object_id] if self._space.labels else self.object_id
        return f"SimilarityPoint({label})"

class SimilarityCenter(SimilarityPoint, Center):
    def brownian_motion(self, time_to_travel: float):
        """Random jump to nearby object."""
        # Find most similar objects and randomly jump to one
        similarities = self._space.similarity_matrix[self.object_id]
        top_similar = np.argsort(similarities)[-5:]  # Top 5 most similar
        self.object_id = np.random.choice(top_similar)
    
    def drift(self, target_point: Point, prop_to_travel: float):
        """Move toward most similar object to target."""
        if not isinstance(target_point, SimilarityPoint):
            return
        
        # With some probability, jump to target or similar object
        if np.random.random() < prop_to_travel:
            target_similarities = self._space.similarity_matrix[target_point.object_id]
            most_similar = np.argmax(target_similarities)
            self.object_id = most_similar

# Test similarity space
similarity_data = np.array([
    [1.0, 0.9, 0.1, 0.2, 0.1],
    [0.9, 1.0, 0.2, 0.1, 0.2], 
    [0.1, 0.2, 1.0, 0.8, 0.9],
    [0.2, 0.1, 0.8, 1.0, 0.7],
    [0.1, 0.2, 0.9, 0.7, 1.0]
])

labels = ['Doc1', 'Doc2', 'Doc3', 'Doc4', 'Doc5']
sim_space = SimilaritySpace(similarity_data, labels)

# Generate points (each object appears multiple times)
sim_points = []
for obj_id in range(5):
    for _ in range(4):  # 4 copies of each object
        sim_points.append(SimilarityPoint(sim_space, obj_id))

print(f"Similarity points: {sim_points[:10]}")

# Cluster in similarity space
sa_sim = SimulatedAnnealing(sim_points, k=2)
sim_centers = sa_sim.run(robust_prop=0.1)
sim_energy = sa_sim.calculate_energy(sim_centers, sim_points)

print(f"Similarity clustering energy: {sim_energy:.3f}")
print(f"Final centers: {sim_centers}")

Similarity points: [SimilarityPoint(Doc1), SimilarityPoint(Doc1), SimilarityPoint(Doc1), SimilarityPoint(Doc1), SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc2), SimilarityPoint(Doc3), SimilarityPoint(Doc3)]
Similarity clustering energy: 0.258
Final centers: [SimilarityPoint(Doc4), SimilarityPoint(Doc5)]

title: Custom Metric Spaces
text:
Best Practices and Performance Tips

Implementation Guidelines

# Performance and correctness checklist
def validate_custom_space(space_class, n_test_points=50):
    """Validate a custom metric space implementation."""
    print(f"Validating {space_class.__name__}...")
    
    try:
        # Test instantiation
        space = space_class()
        print("✓ Space instantiation successful")
        
        # Test point sampling
        points = space.sample_points(n_test_points)
        print(f"✓ Point sampling: {len(points)} points generated")
        
        # Test center sampling  
        centers = space.sample_centers(3)
        print(f"✓ Center sampling: {len(centers)} centers generated")
        
        # Test distance computation
        if len(points) >= 2:
            dist = space.distance(points[0], points[1])
            print(f"✓ Distance computation: d = {dist:.3f}")
            
            # Check metric properties
            # 1. Non-negativity
            assert dist >= 0, "Distance must be non-negative"
            
            # 2. Identity of indiscernibles  
            self_dist = space.distance(points[0], points[0])
            assert abs(self_dist) < 1e-10, "Distance to self must be zero"
            
            # 3. Symmetry
            reverse_dist = space.distance(points[1], points[0])
            assert abs(dist - reverse_dist) < 1e-10, "Distance must be symmetric"
            print("✓ Metric properties validated")
        
        # Test clustering integration
        from kmeanssa_ng import SimulatedAnnealing
        sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points[:20], k=2)
        result_centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.05)
        energy = sa.calculate_energy(result_centers, points[:20])
        print(f"✓ Clustering integration: final energy = {energy:.3f}")
        
        print(f"✅ {space_class.__name__} validation successful!\n")
        return True
        
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"❌ {space_class.__name__} validation failed: {e}\n")
        return False

title: Quantum Graphs
text: Quantum graphs are the foundation of kmeanssa-ng. This guide provides everything you need to know about working with quantum graphs effectively.

title: Quantum Graphs
text:
What Makes Quantum Graphs Special?
Unlike traditional graphs where vertices represent discrete entities, quantum graphs treat edges as first-class citizens:

Points can exist anywhere on an edge, not just at vertices
Distance is measured along the graph structure (geodesic distance)
Each edge has a length attribute representing physical distance
The graph must be connected for distance computation


from kmeanssa_ng import QuantumGraph, QGPoint

# Create a quantum graph manually
graph = QuantumGraph()
graph.add_edge(0, 1, length=2.0)  # Edge with length 2
graph.add_edge(1, 2, length=1.5)  # Edge with length 1.5
graph.add_edge(2, 0, length=3.0)  # Edge with length 3

print(f"Manual graph: {graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {graph.number_of_edges()} edges")

Manual graph: 3 nodes, 3 edges



# Points can be positioned anywhere on edges
point_a = QGPoint(graph, edge=(0, 1), position=0.25)  # 25% along edge (0,1)
point_b = QGPoint(graph, edge=(1, 2), position=0.80)  # 80% along edge (1,2)

print(f"Point A: {point_a}")
print(f"Point B: {point_b}")

# Distance is computed along the graph
graph.precomputing()  # Essential for distance computation
distance = graph.distance(point_a, point_b)
print(f"Distance: {distance:.3f}")

Point A: QGPoint on edge (0, 1) at position 0.250
Point B: QGPoint on edge (1, 2) at position 0.800
Distance: 2.550

Creating Quantum Graphs

QGPoint - Data Points
Points represent data locations on the quantum graph:

print(f"Point1 position: {point1.position}")

Hash of point1: 282291444
Point1 edge: (0, 2)
Point1 position: 0.3




QGCenter - Cluster Centers
Centers can move during the clustering process:

from kmeanssa_ng import QGCenter

# Create a center from a QGPoint using an existing edge
simple_edges = list(simple_graph.edges())[:3]
print(f"Simple graph edges: {simple_edges}")

initial_point = QGPoint(simple_graph, edge=simple_edges[0], position=0.5)
center = QGCenter(initial_point)
print(f"Initial center: {center}")

# Centers can perform operations (these modify the center in place)
h = 0.1  # Step size for Brownian motion
center.brownian_motion(h)
print(f"After Brownian motion: {center}")

# Get the closest node (useful for interpretation)
closest_node = center._closest_node()
print(f"Center closest to node: {closest_node}")

title: Quantum Graphs
text:
Distance Computation
Understanding how distances are computed is crucial for effective use:

Same Edge Distance

# Two points on the same edge
first_edge = available_edges[0]
same_edge_point1 = QGPoint(sbm_graph, edge=first_edge, position=0.2)  # 20% along edge
same_edge_point2 = QGPoint(sbm_graph, edge=first_edge, position=0.8)  # 80% along edge

# Distance = |0.8 - 0.2| * edge_length
same_edge_distance = sbm_graph.distance(same_edge_point1, same_edge_point2)
edge_length = sbm_graph[first_edge[0]][first_edge[1]]['length']
expected_distance = abs(0.8 - 0.2) * edge_length

print(f"Same edge distance: {same_edge_distance:.3f}")
print(f"Expected distance: {expected_distance:.3f}")
print(f"Edge length: {edge_length:.3f}")

Same edge distance: 0.600
Expected distance: 0.600
Edge length: 1.000




Different Edge Distance
For points on different edges, the algorithm finds the shortest path:

# Points on different edges
diff_edge_point1 = QGPoint(sbm_graph, edge=available_edges[0], position=0.3)
diff_edge_point2 = QGPoint(sbm_graph, edge=available_edges[2], position=0.7)

# Complex geodesic distance computation
diff_edge_distance = sbm_graph.distance(diff_edge_point1, diff_edge_point2)
print(f"Different edges distance: {diff_edge_distance:.3f}")

# This involves finding shortest paths between nodes and combining distances

Different edges distance: 1.000




Performance: Precomputing
Always call precomputing() before intensive distance calculations:

import time
from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm

# Create a test graph for timing
timing_graph = generate_sbm(sizes=[15, 15], p=[[0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8]])
timing_points = timing_graph.sample_points(20)

# Without precomputing (slower)
start_time = time.time()
distance_no_cache = timing_graph.distance(timing_points[0], timing_points[1])
time_no_cache = time.time() - start_time

# With precomputing (faster)
timing_graph.precomputing()
start_time = time.time()
distance_with_cache = timing_graph.distance(timing_points[0], timing_points[1])
time_with_cache = time.time() - start_time

print(f"Without precomputing: {distance_no_cache:.3f} (time: {time_no_cache:.6f}s)")
print(f"With precomputing: {distance_with_cache:.3f} (time: {time_with_cache:.6f}s)")
speedup = time_no_cache / time_with_cache if time_with_cache > 0 else float('inf')
print(f"Speedup: {speedup:.1f}x faster")

Without precomputing: 1.000 (time: 0.000047s)
With precomputing: 1.000 (time: 0.000034s)
Speedup: 1.4x faster

import random

def custom_sample_points(graph, n_points):

title: Core Concepts
text: Understanding the fundamental concepts behind kmeanssa-ng will help you use the library effectively and extend it for your own research.

title: Core Concepts
text:
What are Quantum Graphs?
A quantum graph is a metric graph where:

Nodes are connected by edges with positive lengths
Points can exist anywhere on edges, not just at nodes

Distance is measured along the graph (geodesic distance)


from kmeanssa_ng import QuantumGraph, QGPoint

# Create a simple triangle graph
graph = QuantumGraph()
graph.add_edge(0, 1, length=1.0)
graph.add_edge(1, 2, length=1.5) 
graph.add_edge(2, 0, length=2.0)

print(f"Triangle graph: {graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {graph.number_of_edges()} edges")

Triangle graph: 3 nodes, 3 edges



# Points can be anywhere on edges
point_a = QGPoint(graph, edge=(0, 1), position=0.3)  # 30% along edge (0,1)
point_b = QGPoint(graph, edge=(1, 2), position=0.8)  # 80% along edge (1,2)

print(f"Point A: {point_a}")
print(f"Point B: {point_b}")

# Distance is measured along the graph
graph.precomputing()  # Essential for distance calculations
distance = graph.distance(point_a, point_b)
print(f"Distance between points: {distance:.3f}")

Point A: QGPoint on edge (0, 1) at position 0.300
Point B: QGPoint on edge (1, 2) at position 0.800
Distance between points: 1.500



Why Quantum Graphs?
Traditional clustering algorithms assume points live in Euclidean spaces (like 2D or 3D). But many real-world scenarios involve:

Road networks - GPS coordinates along roads
Neural networks - Signals propagating along connections

Social networks - Information spreading through connections
River networks - Pollution or species distribution along waterways

title: Core Concepts
text:
Simulated Annealing for K-means
The core algorithm combines two processes:

1. Brownian Motion (Exploration)
Centers perform random walks to explore the space:
Purpose: Avoid getting stuck in local minima by exploring widely.


2. Drift (Exploitation)
Centers move toward their nearest data points:
Purpose: Refine center positions based on actual data.
Let’s see this in action:

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, QGSimulatedAnnealing, SimulatedAnnealing, QGPoint

# Create a simple 2-cluster graph for demonstration
demo_graph = generate_sbm(sizes=[20, 20], p=[[0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8]])
demo_graph.precomputing()
demo_points = demo_graph.sample_points(50)

print(f"Demo graph: {demo_graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {len(demo_points)} points")

Demo graph: 40 nodes, 50 points




Temperature Control
The balance between exploration and exploitation is controlled by temperature:

High temperature → More Brownian motion (exploration)
Low temperature → More drift (exploitation)


# High temperature (more exploration)
high_temp_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2, lambda_param=3.0, beta=0.5)
high_temp_centers = high_temp_sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)

# Low temperature (more exploitation)
low_temp_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2, lambda_param=0.5, beta=2.0)
low_temp_centers = low_temp_sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)

print(f"High temperature centers: {high_temp_centers}")
print(f"Low temperature centers: {low_temp_centers}")

High temperature centers: [30, 17]
Low temperature centers: [30, 18]


Temperature decreases over time using an inhomogeneous Poisson process.

title: Core Concepts
text:
Algorithm Variants

Version 1 (v1) - Interleaved
For each iteration: brownian_motion() → drift_step()


Version 2 (v2) - Sequential
First half iterations: pure exploration, second half: pure exploitation

# Compare algorithm versions
v1_sa = SimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)
v1_centers = v1_sa.run(algorithm_version="v1", initialization="kpp")

v2_sa = SimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)  
v2_centers = v2_sa.run(algorithm_version="v2", initialization="kpp")

print(f"Version 1 found {len(v1_centers)} centers")
print(f"Version 2 found {len(v2_centers)} centers")

Version 1 found 2 centers
Version 2 found 2 centers


Choose based on your problem: - v1: Balanced for most cases - v2: Better when you need distinct exploration/exploitation phases

title: Core Concepts
text:
Robustification
Instead of using just the final result, kmeanssa-ng averages the last few iterations:

# Compare different robustification levels
robust_levels = [0.05, 0.1, 0.2]  # 5%, 10%, 20%

print("Robustification comparison:")
for prop in robust_levels:
    sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)
    centers = sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=prop)
    print(f"  {prop*100:2.0f}% robustification: {centers}")

Robustification comparison:
   5% robustification: [3, 15]
  10% robustification: [9, 39]
  20% robustification: [37, 18]


Benefits: - Reduces sensitivity to random fluctuations - More stable and reproducible results - Better convergence in noisy scenarios

title: Core Concepts
text:
Key Parameters

Algorithm Parameters

k: Number of clusters (like standard k-means)
lambda_param: Temperature parameter (higher = more exploration)
beta: Drift strength (higher = stronger attraction to data)

step_size: Random walk step size



Tuning Guidelines

# Parameter sensitivity analysis
params_to_test = [
    {"lambda_param": 2.0, "beta": 0.5, "name": "Conservative (more exploration)"},
    {"lambda_param": 1.0, "beta": 1.0, "name": "Balanced"},
    {"lambda_param": 0.5, "beta": 2.0, "name": "Aggressive (more exploitation)"},
]

print("Parameter tuning results:")
for params in params_to_test:
    sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2, 
                             lambda_param=params["lambda_param"], 
                             beta=params["beta"])
    centers = sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)
    print(f"  {params['name']}: {centers}")

Parameter tuning results:
  Conservative (more exploration): [21, 13]
  Balanced: [38, 3]
  Aggressive (more exploitation): [18, 27]



# Fine-grained control with step size
step_sizes = [0.05, 0.1, 0.2]
print("\nStep size comparison:")
for step_size in step_sizes:
    sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2, step_size=step_size)
    centers = sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)
    print(f"  Step size {step_size}: {centers}")


Step size comparison:
  Step size 0.05: [10, 30]
  Step size 0.1: [23, 16]
  Step size 0.2: [8, 19]

title: Core Concepts
text:
Initialization Strategies

Random vs k-means++ Initialization

# Compare initialization strategies
init_methods = ["random", "kpp"]
print("Initialization comparison:")

for method in init_methods:
    sa = SimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)
    centers = sa.run(initialization=method, robust_prop=0.1)
    energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, demo_points)
    print(f"  {method:6s}: energy = {energy:.3f}")

Initialization comparison:
  random: energy = 2.233
  kpp   : energy = 1.594


k-means++ spreads initial centers apart, leading to: - Faster convergence - Better final results - More consistent outcomes

title: Core Concepts
text:
Distance Computation
Quantum graphs use geodesic distance - the shortest path along the graph:

# Demonstrate distance calculation
# To ensure the example is robust, pick existing edges from the random graph
edge1 = list(demo_graph.edges)[0]
edge2 = list(demo_graph.edges)[-1] # Pick the last edge to increase chance of being different

# Create two points on different edges
point1 = QGPoint(demo_graph, edge=edge1, position=0.2)
point2 = QGPoint(demo_graph, edge=edge2, position=0.7)

distance = demo_graph.distance(point1, point2)
print(f"Geodesic distance between points on different edges: {distance:.3f}")

# Compare with points on same edge
point3 = QGPoint(demo_graph, edge=edge1, position=0.8)
same_edge_distance = demo_graph.distance(point1, point3)
print(f"Distance between points on same edge: {same_edge_distance:.3f}")

Geodesic distance between points on different edges: 2.500
Distance between points on same edge: 0.600


Performance tip: Always call graph.precomputing() to cache shortest paths between all node pairs.

title: Core Concepts
text:
Quantum Graph Specific Features
The QGSimulatedAnnealing class provides quantum graph specific methods:

# Quantum graph specific clustering
qg_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)

# Get results as node IDs (more interpretable)
node_centers = qg_sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)
print(f"Centers near nodes: {node_centers}")

# Find overall mean (k=1 case)
mean_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=1)
mean_node = mean_sa.run_for_mean(robust_prop=0.1)
print(f"Overall mean near node: {mean_node}")

# Compare with exact positions
regular_sa = SimulatedAnnealing(demo_points, k=2)
exact_centers = regular_sa.run(initialization="kpp")
print(f"Exact center positions: {[str(c) for c in exact_centers]}")

Centers near nodes: [37, 13]
Overall mean near node: 37
Exact center positions: ["QGPoint on 'stochastic_block_model' edge (30, 27) at position 0.000", "QGPoint on 'stochastic_block_model' edge (1, 6) at position 0.000"]

text:
Extending to New Spaces
The three-layer architecture makes it easy to add new metric spaces:
from kmeanssa_ng.core import Space, Point, Center

class MySpace(Space):
    def distance(self, p1: Point, p2: Point) -> float:
        # Implement your distance function
        pass
    
    def sample_points(self, n: int) -> list[Point]:
        # Implement point sampling  
        pass
    
    # ... implement other required methods

# Use the same SimulatedAnnealing algorithm
sa = SimulatedAnnealing(my_points, k=3)
centers = sa.run()

title: Core Concepts
text:
Performance Considerations

# Performance tips demonstration
import time

# Without precomputing (slower)
slow_graph = generate_sbm(sizes=[15, 15], p=[[0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8]])
slow_points = slow_graph.sample_points(30)

start_time = time.time()
# Note: This would be much slower on larger graphs without precomputing
distance_without_cache = slow_graph.distance(slow_points[0], slow_points[1])
time_without_cache = time.time() - start_time

# With precomputing (faster)
slow_graph.precomputing()
start_time = time.time()
distance_with_cache = slow_graph.distance(slow_points[0], slow_points[1])
time_with_cache = time.time() - start_time

print(f"Distance calculation:")
print(f"  Without precomputing: {distance_without_cache:.3f} (time: {time_without_cache:.6f}s)")
print(f"  With precomputing: {distance_with_cache:.3f} (time: {time_with_cache:.6f}s)")
print(f"  Speedup: {time_without_cache / time_with_cache:.1f}x faster")

Distance calculation:
  Without precomputing: 3.000 (time: 0.000061s)
  With precomputing: 3.000 (time: 0.000035s)
  Speedup: 1.7x faster

title: Basic Clustering Examples
text: This guide provides practical examples of using kmeanssa-ng for various clustering scenarios.

title: Basic Clustering Examples
text:
Example 1: Two-Cluster Stochastic Block Model
The most basic scenario - clustering points on a graph with clear community structure:

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, QGSimulatedAnnealing, SimulatedAnnealing

# Generate a graph with two distinct communities
graph = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[40, 40],       # Two communities of 40 nodes each
    p=[[0.8, 0.1],        # High intra-community connectivity  
       [0.1, 0.8]],       # Low inter-community connectivity
)

# Essential: precompute shortest paths
graph.precomputing()

# Sample points uniformly across the graph
points = graph.sample_points(150)

print(f"Created graph with {graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes and {graph.number_of_edges()} edges")
print(f"Sampled {len(points)} points on the graph")

Created graph with 80 nodes and 1393 edges
Sampled 150 points on the graph



# Run quantum graph specialized simulated annealing
qg_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(
    observations=points,
    k=2,                  # We know there are 2 clusters
    lambda_param=1.0,     # Standard temperature
    beta=1.0,            # Standard drift strength
    step_size=0.1        # Standard step size
)

# Get cluster centers as node IDs (more interpretable)
node_centers = qg_sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)
print(f"Cluster centers near nodes: {node_centers}")

# For comparison, exact positions with base class
sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2)
centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.1, initialization="kpp")
print(f"Found {len(centers)} exact centers")

Cluster centers near nodes: [70, 0]
Found 2 exact centers



# Display results
print("\nQuantum Graph specific results:")
for i, node_id in enumerate(node_centers):
    print(f"  Cluster {i+1} center near node: {node_id}")

print("\nExact center positions:")
for i, center in enumerate(centers[:2]):  # Show first 2
    print(f"  Cluster {i+1}: {center}")


Quantum Graph specific results:
  Cluster 1 center near node: 70
  Cluster 2 center near node: 0

Exact center positions:
  Cluster 1: QGPoint on 'stochastic_block_model' edge (74, 46) at position 0.000
  Cluster 2: QGPoint on 'stochastic_block_model' edge (26, 14) at position 0.000

title: Basic Clustering Examples
text:
Example 2: Multi-Cluster Complex Graph
Handling more complex scenarios with multiple clusters:

# Create a 4-cluster graph with varying connectivity
graph_4 = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[20, 30, 25, 35],  # Different cluster sizes
    p=[[0.9, 0.1, 0.05, 0.02],  # Strong within-cluster connectivity
       [0.1, 0.8, 0.15, 0.05],  # Moderate cross-cluster connections
       [0.05, 0.15, 0.85, 0.1], 
       [0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.9]]
)

graph_4.precomputing()
points_4 = graph_4.sample_points(200)

print(f"4-cluster graph: {graph_4.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {graph_4.number_of_edges()} edges")

4-cluster graph: 110 nodes, 1687 edges



# For more clusters, you might need more exploration
qg_sa_4 = QGSimulatedAnnealing(
    observations=points_4,
    k=4,
    lambda_param=1.5,     # Slightly higher temperature for exploration
    beta=0.8,            # Slightly lower drift for broader search
    step_size=0.15       # Slightly larger steps
)

node_centers_4 = qg_sa_4.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.15)
print(f"4-cluster result: {len(node_centers_4)} centers near nodes: {node_centers_4}")

4-cluster result: 4 centers near nodes: [46, 17, 12, 61]

title: Basic Clustering Examples
text:
Example 3: Using Existing NetworkX Graphs
Convert real-world networks to quantum graphs:

import networkx as nx
from kmeanssa_ng import as_quantum_graph

# Start with a well-known network
G = nx.karate_club_graph()

# Convert to quantum graph with uniform edge lengths
karate_graph = as_quantum_graph(G, edge_length=1.0)
karate_graph.precomputing()

print(f"Karate club graph: {karate_graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {karate_graph.number_of_edges()} edges")

Karate club graph: 34 nodes, 78 edges



# Sample points and cluster
karate_points = karate_graph.sample_points(50)
qg_sa_karate = QGSimulatedAnnealing(karate_points, k=2)  # Karate club has 2 known communities
karate_nodes = qg_sa_karate.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)

print(f"Karate club clustering centers near nodes: {karate_nodes}")

Karate club clustering centers near nodes: [20, 0]



# You can also use custom edge lengths based on graph properties
G_weighted = nx.karate_club_graph()

# Add custom weights (e.g., inverse of edge betweenness)
betweenness = nx.edge_betweenness_centrality(G_weighted)
for (u, v), centrality in betweenness.items():
    G_weighted[u][v]['length'] = 1.0 / (centrality + 0.01)  # Avoid division by zero

# Convert to quantum graph first, then update lengths
karate_weighted = as_quantum_graph(G_weighted, edge_length=1.0)  # Default lengths
# Update with custom lengths
for (u, v), centrality in betweenness.items():
    karate_weighted[u][v]['length'] = 1.0 / (centrality + 0.01)
    
karate_weighted.precomputing()
print(f"Weighted karate club graph created successfully")

Weighted karate club graph created successfully

title: Basic Clustering Examples
text:
Example 4: Parameter Sensitivity Analysis
Understanding how parameters affect clustering:

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_simple_graph

# Create a symmetric test graph
simple_graph = generate_simple_graph(n_a=10, n_aa=5, bridge_length=3.0)
simple_graph.precomputing()
simple_points = simple_graph.sample_points(50)

print(f"Simple test graph: {simple_graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes")

Simple test graph: 122 nodes



# Test different parameter combinations
parameters = [
    {"lambda_param": 0.5, "beta": 2.0, "name": "Low exploration, high drift"},
    {"lambda_param": 1.0, "beta": 1.0, "name": "Balanced"},
    {"lambda_param": 2.0, "beta": 0.5, "name": "High exploration, low drift"},
]

results = []
for params in parameters:
    qg_sa_test = QGSimulatedAnnealing(
        simple_points, k=2, 
        lambda_param=params["lambda_param"], 
        beta=params["beta"]
    )
    node_centers_test = qg_sa_test.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)
    
    # Calculate energy using base class for comparison
    sa_test = SimulatedAnnealing(simple_points, k=2, 
                                lambda_param=params["lambda_param"], 
                                beta=params["beta"])
    centers_test = sa_test.run(initialization="kpp")
    energy = sa_test.calculate_energy(centers_test, simple_points)
    
    results.append({
        "name": params["name"],
        "energy": energy,
        "node_centers": node_centers_test
    })

# Compare results
print("Parameter sensitivity analysis:")
for result in results:
    print(f"{result['name']}: Energy = {result['energy']:.3f}, Nodes = {result['node_centers']}")

Parameter sensitivity analysis:
Low exploration, high drift: Energy = 5.194, Nodes = ['A4', 'B81']
Balanced: Energy = 6.022, Nodes = ['A0', 'B0']
High exploration, low drift: Energy = 4.847, Nodes = ['B8', 'A10']

text:
Example 5: Mean Finding (k=1 case)
Finding the overall center using quantum graph specific features:

# Use the 2-cluster graph from Example 1
print("Finding overall mean center (k=1):")

# For finding the overall mean (k=1 case)  
mean_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points, k=1)
mean_node = mean_sa.run_for_mean(robust_prop=0.1)

Finding overall mean center (k=1):
Mean center near node: 76
2-cluster centers near nodes: [70, 0]

title: Basic Clustering Examples
text:
Example 6: Batch Processing and Reproducibility
Processing multiple graphs and ensuring reproducible results:

import numpy as np
from kmeanssa_ng import generate_random_sbm

# Set random seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Process multiple random graphs
results = []
for trial in range(3):  # Reduced for demo
    # Generate random SBM with different edge lengths
    graph_random = generate_random_sbm(
        sizes=[25, 25], 
        p=[[0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8]],
        lengths=[[1.0, 3.0], [3.0, 1.0]]  # Different intra/inter lengths
    )
    
    graph_random.precomputing()
    points_random = graph_random.sample_points(80)
    
    # Use quantum graph method
    qg_sa_random = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points_random, k=2)
    node_centers_random = qg_sa_random.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)
    
    # Calculate energy for comparison
    sa_random = SimulatedAnnealing(points_random, k=2)
    centers_random = sa_random.run(initialization="kpp")
    energy = sa_random.calculate_energy(centers_random, points_random)
    
    results.append({
        "trial": trial + 1,
        "energy": energy,
        "node_centers": node_centers_random
    })

# Analyze results
energies = [r["energy"] for r in results]
print(f"Energy statistics across {len(results)} trials:")
print(f"  Mean: {np.mean(energies):.3f}")
print(f"  Std:  {np.std(energies):.3f}")
print(f"  Min:  {np.min(energies):.3f}")
print(f"  Max:  {np.max(energies):.3f}")

Energy statistics across 3 trials:
  Mean: 1.363
  Std:  0.203
  Min:  1.075
  Max:  1.512

title: Graph Generators
text: Graph generators are essential for testing, benchmarking, and experimenting with clustering algorithms. This guide covers all the graph generation functions in kmeanssa-ng, from simple test cases to complex stochastic models.

title: Graph Generators
text:
Overview
The library provides several types of graph generators:

Simple graphs: Two-cluster test cases for development
Stochastic Block Models (SBM): Multi-cluster graphs with controllable structure
NetworkX converters: Transform existing graphs to quantum graphs
Complete graphs: Dense graphs from similarity matrices


import numpy as np
import networkx as nx
from kmeanssa_ng import (
    generate_simple_graph, 
    generate_sbm, 
    generate_random_sbm,
    as_quantum_graph,
    complete_quantum_graph
)

print("Graph generators loaded successfully!")

Graph generators loaded successfully!

title: Graph Generators
text:
Simple Graphs

Basic Two-Cluster Graph
The simplest generator creates symmetric two-cluster graphs connected by a bridge:

# Create a basic two-cluster graph
simple_graph = generate_simple_graph(
    n_a=5,              # 5 neighbors for each central node
    n_aa=3,             # 3 second-level neighbors for each first-level node  
    bridge_length=2.0   # Distance between clusters
)

print(f"Simple graph: {simple_graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {simple_graph.number_of_edges()} edges")
print(f"Cluster A central node: A0")
print(f"Cluster B central node: B0")
print(f"Bridge length: {simple_graph.get_edge_data('A0', 'B0')['length']}")

# Graph comes pre-computed and ready to use
points = simple_graph.sample_points(50)
print(f"Sampled {len(points)} points for clustering")

Simple graph: 42 nodes, 41 edges
Cluster A central node: A0
Cluster B central node: B0
Bridge length: 2.0
Sampled 50 points for clustering




Graph Structure Analysis

# Examine the structure
print("Node naming pattern:")
print(f"A cluster nodes: {[n for n in simple_graph.nodes() if n.startswith('A')][:10]}...")
print(f"B cluster nodes: {[n for n in simple_graph.nodes() if n.startswith('B')][:10]}...")

# Check attributes
node_weights = nx.get_node_attributes(simple_graph, 'weight')
edge_lengths = nx.get_edge_attributes(simple_graph, 'length')

print(f"\nAll nodes have weight 1: {all(w == 1 for w in node_weights.values())}")
print(f"Most edges have length 1: {sum(1 for l in edge_lengths.values() if l == 1.0)} of {len(edge_lengths)}")
print(f"Bridge edges: {[(u,v,d['length']) for u,v,d in simple_graph.edges(data=True) if d['length'] != 1.0]}")

Node naming pattern:
A cluster nodes: ['A0', 'A1', 'A11', 'A12', 'A13', 'A2', 'A21', 'A22', 'A23', 'A3']...
B cluster nodes: ['B0', 'B1', 'B11', 'B12', 'B13', 'B2', 'B21', 'B22', 'B23', 'B3']...

All nodes have weight 1: True
Most edges have length 1: 40 of 41
Bridge edges: [('A0', 'B0', 2.0)]




Parameter Effects

# Compare different parameter settings
configs = [
    {"n_a": 3, "n_aa": 2, "bridge_length": 1.0},
    {"n_a": 5, "n_aa": 3, "bridge_length": 3.0}, 
    {"n_a": 8, "n_aa": 2, "bridge_length": 5.0}
]

for i, config in enumerate(configs):
    graph = generate_simple_graph(**config)
    print(f"Config {i+1}: n_a={config['n_a']}, n_aa={config['n_aa']}, bridge={config['bridge_length']}")
    print(f"  → {graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {graph.number_of_edges()} edges")
    print(f"  → Diameter: {graph.diameter:.2f}")

Config 1: n_a=3, n_aa=2, bridge=1.0
  → 20 nodes, 19 edges
  → Diameter: 5.00
Config 2: n_a=5, n_aa=3, bridge=3.0
  → 42 nodes, 41 edges
  → Diameter: 7.00

title: Graph Generators
text:
Stochastic Block Models

Basic SBM
Stochastic Block Models create multi-cluster graphs with controllable intra- and inter-cluster connectivity:

# Create a two-block SBM
sbm_graph = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[40, 60],                    # 40 nodes in block 1, 60 in block 2
    p=[[0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.7]]       # High intra-block, low inter-block probability
)

print(f"SBM graph: {sbm_graph.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {sbm_graph.number_of_edges()} edges")
print(f"Expected edges within block 1: ~{40*39/2 * 0.8:.0f}")
print(f"Expected edges within block 2: ~{60*59/2 * 0.7:.0f}")  
print(f"Expected edges between blocks: ~{40*60 * 0.1:.0f}")

SBM graph: 100 nodes, 2133 edges
Expected edges within block 1: ~624
Expected edges within block 2: ~1239
Expected edges between blocks: ~240




Multi-Block SBM

# Create a three-block SBM with different cluster strengths
three_block_sbm = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[30, 25, 35],
    p=[[0.9, 0.05, 0.02],   # Block 1: very tight
       [0.05, 0.7, 0.15],    # Block 2: medium, some connection to block 3
       [0.02, 0.15, 0.8]]    # Block 3: tight, connected to block 2
)

print(f"Three-block SBM: {three_block_sbm.number_of_nodes()} nodes")

# Check block assignments
block_attr = nx.get_node_attributes(three_block_sbm, 'block')
block_counts = {}
for node, block in block_attr.items():
    block_counts[block] = block_counts.get(block, 0) + 1

print(f"Block sizes: {dict(sorted(block_counts.items()))}")

Three-block SBM: 90 nodes
Block sizes: {0: 30, 1: 25, 2: 35}




SBM with Custom Edge Lengths

# Create SBM with different intra/inter-cluster distances
custom_sbm = generate_random_sbm(
    sizes=[50, 50],
    p=[[0.7, 0.1], [0.1, 0.7]],
    weights=[1, 1],                    # Equal node weights
    lengths=[[1.0, 4.0], [4.0, 1.0]]  # Long inter-cluster edges
)

print(f"Custom SBM: {custom_sbm.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {custom_sbm.number_of_edges()} edges")

# Analyze edge length distribution

✗ Empty SBM sizes: sizes must be a non-empty list...
✗ Invalid probability: p[0][0] must be in [0, 1], got 1.5...




Graph Validation

# Validate generated graphs
def validate_quantum_graph(graph, name):
    """Validate that a quantum graph is properly constructed."""
    issues = []
    
    # Check connectivity
    if not nx.is_connected(graph):
        issues.append("Graph not connected")
    
    # Check edge lengths
    try:
        graph.validate_edge_lengths()
    except ValueError as e:
        issues.append(f"Edge validation failed: {e}")
    
    # Check node weights
    node_weights = nx.get_node_attributes(graph, 'weight')
    if not all(w > 0 for w in node_weights.values()):
        issues.append("Some node weights are not positive")
    
    # Check if precomputing works
    try:
        graph.precomputing() 
    except Exception as e:
        issues.append(f"Precomputing failed: {e}")
    
    if issues:
        print(f"⚠️  {name}: {', '.join(issues)}")
    else:
        print(f"✅ {name}: Valid quantum graph")

# Test all generator types
test_graphs = [
    ("Simple graph", generate_simple_graph()),
    ("SBM graph", generate_sbm()),
    ("Random SBM", generate_random_sbm()),
    ("Converted NetworkX", as_quantum_graph(nx.path_graph(5)))
]

for name, graph in test_graphs:
    validate_quantum_graph(graph, name)

title: Simulated Annealing
text: The simulated annealing algorithm is the heart of kmeanssa-ng. This guide explains how to use it effectively for clustering on quantum graphs and other metric spaces.

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Understanding the Algorithm
The simulated annealing approach solves the k-means problem on arbitrary metric spaces by combining: - Brownian motion: Random exploration to avoid local minima - Drift toward observations: Exploitation to find optimal cluster centers - Temperature control: Using an inhomogeneous Poisson process

from kmeanssa_ng import generate_sbm, SimulatedAnnealing
import numpy as np

# Create a test graph with clear cluster structure
graph = generate_sbm(
    sizes=[30, 30], 
    p=[[0.8, 0.1], [0.1, 0.8]]
)
graph.precomputing()

# Sample points from the graph
points = graph.sample_points(100)
print(f"Created {len(points)} points on a graph with {len(graph.nodes())} nodes")

Created 100 points on a graph with 60 nodes

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Basic Usage

Simple Clustering

# Create simulated annealing instance
sa = SimulatedAnnealing(
    observations=points,
    k=2,  # Number of clusters
    lambda_param=1,    # Poisson intensity parameter
    beta=1.0,          # Inverse temperature parameter
    step_size=0.1      # Time step size
)

# Run the algorithm
centers = sa.run(
    robust_prop=0.1,        # Use 10% of iterations for robustification
    initialization="kpp"    # Use k-means++ initialization
)

print(f"Found {len(centers)} cluster centers")
print(f"Centers are of type: {type(centers[0])}")

Found 2 cluster centers
Centers are of type: <class 'kmeanssa_ng.quantum_graph.center.QGCenter'>




Energy Evaluation

# Calculate the clustering energy (average squared distance to nearest center)
energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
print(f"Final clustering energy: {energy:.3f}")

# Compare with random centers
random_centers = graph.sample_centers(2)
random_energy = sa.calculate_energy(random_centers, points)
print(f"Random centers energy: {random_energy:.3f}")
print(f"Improvement: {random_energy - energy:.3f}")

Final clustering energy: 0.998
Random centers energy: 2.130
Improvement: 1.132

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Algorithm Parameters

Core Parameters

k: Number of clusters (must be > 0)
lambda_param: Controls the intensity of the Poisson process (must be > 0)
beta: Inverse temperature parameter - higher values lead to faster convergence (must be > 0)
step_size: Time step for the algorithm (must be > 0)


# Example with different parameter settings
parameters = [
    {"lambda_param": 0.5, "beta": 0.5, "step_size": 0.05},
    {"lambda_param": 1.0, "beta": 1.0, "step_size": 0.1},
    {"lambda_param": 2.0, "beta": 2.0, "step_size": 0.2}
]

for i, params in enumerate(parameters):
    sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2, **params)
    centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.1, initialization="kpp")
    energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
    print(f"Config {i+1}: λ={params['lambda_param']}, β={params['beta']}, h={params['step_size']} → energy={energy:.3f}")

Config 1: λ=0.5, β=0.5, h=0.05 → energy=0.916
Config 2: λ=1.0, β=1.0, h=0.1 → energy=1.242
Config 3: λ=2.0, β=2.0, h=0.2 → energy=1.637

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Initialization Methods

K-means++ (Recommended)
K-means++ provides smart initialization by choosing centers that are far apart:

# K-means++ initialization (default)
sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=3)
centers_kpp = sa.run(initialization="kpp")
energy_kpp = sa.calculate_energy(centers_kpp, points)
print(f"K-means++ energy: {energy_kpp:.3f}")

K-means++ energy: 1.629




Random Initialization

# Random initialization
centers_random = sa.run(initialization="random")
energy_random = sa.calculate_energy(centers_random, points)
print(f"Random initialization energy: {energy_random:.3f}")
print(f"K-means++ advantage: {energy_random - energy_kpp:.3f}")

Random initialization energy: 1.030
K-means++ advantage: -0.599

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Algorithm Versions
The library provides two algorithm variants:

Version 1 (Default): Interleaved
Version 1 interleaves Brownian motion and drift steps:

# Run algorithm version 1 (default)
sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2)
centers_v1 = sa.run(algorithm_version="v1")
energy_v1 = sa.calculate_energy(centers_v1, points)
print(f"Algorithm v1 energy: {energy_v1:.3f}")

Algorithm v1 energy: 1.884




Version 2: Sequential
Version 2 performs all Brownian motion first, then drift:

# Run algorithm version 2
centers_v2 = sa.run(algorithm_version="v2")
energy_v2 = sa.calculate_energy(centers_v2, points)
print(f"Algorithm v2 energy: {energy_v2:.3f}")
print(f"Difference: {abs(energy_v1 - energy_v2):.3f}")

Algorithm v2 energy: 1.250
Difference: 0.634

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Robustification
Robustification improves stability by averaging results from the final iterations:

# Test different robustification levels
robust_levels = [0.0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2]
energies = []

for prop in robust_levels:
    sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2)
    centers = sa.run(robust_prop=prop, initialization="kpp")
    energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
    energies.append(energy)
    print(f"Robust prop {prop:.2f}: energy={energy:.3f}")

print(f"Energy variation: {max(energies) - min(energies):.3f}")

Robust prop 0.00: energy=1.159
Robust prop 0.05: energy=1.474
Robust prop 0.10: energy=1.490
Robust prop 0.20: energy=1.215
Energy variation: 0.331

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Quantum Graph Specialization
For quantum graphs, use QGSimulatedAnnealing for additional features:

from kmeanssa_ng import QGSimulatedAnnealing

# Quantum graph specific simulated annealing
qg_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2)

# Standard clustering
centers = qg_sa.run(robust_prop=0.1)
print(f"Standard clustering: {len(centers)} centers")

# Get closest nodes for interpretation
node_ids = qg_sa.run_for_kmeans(robust_prop=0.1)
print(f"Closest nodes: {node_ids}")

Standard clustering: 2 centers
Closest nodes: [58, 14]



Finding the Mean (k=1)

# Find the robust mean (single center)
mean_sa = QGSimulatedAnnealing(points, k=1)
central_node = mean_sa.run_for_mean(robust_prop=0.1)
print(f"Central node ID: {central_node}")

Central node ID: 26

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Performance Optimization

For Large Datasets

# Generate larger test case
large_graph = generate_sbm(sizes=[100, 100, 100], p=[[0.7, 0.1, 0.1], [0.1, 0.7, 0.1], [0.1, 0.1, 0.7]])
large_graph.precomputing()  # Essential for performance
large_points = large_graph.sample_points(500)

# Optimize parameters for larger datasets
sa_large = SimulatedAnnealing(
    large_points, 
    k=3,
    lambda_param=2,     # Higher intensity for faster convergence
    beta=2.0,           # Higher temperature control
    step_size=0.15      # Larger steps
)

centers_large = sa_large.run(
    robust_prop=0.05,   # Smaller robustification for speed
    initialization="kpp"
)

energy_large = sa_large.calculate_energy(centers_large, large_points)
print(f"Large dataset clustering energy: {energy_large:.3f}")

Large dataset clustering energy: 2.038




Memory Considerations

# For very large graphs, work with subsets
n_sample = min(1000, len(large_points))  # Limit sample size
sampled_points = large_points[:n_sample]

sa_memory = SimulatedAnnealing(sampled_points, k=3)
centers_memory = sa_memory.run(robust_prop=0.05)
print(f"Memory-optimized clustering on {len(sampled_points)} points")

Memory-optimized clustering on 500 points

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Practical Applications

Transportation Networks

# Model a transportation network using generated graph
from kmeanssa_ng import generate_simple_graph

# Create a transportation-like network
road_network = generate_simple_graph(
    n_a=10,           # Size of each district
    n_aa=8,           # Connections within districts
    bridge_length=4.0 # Distance between districts
)
road_network.precomputing()

# Sample locations (e.g., customer addresses)
locations = road_network.sample_points(50)

# Find optimal service center locations
sa_transport = SimulatedAnnealing(locations, k=3, beta=1.5)
service_centers = sa_transport.run(initialization="kpp")
transport_energy = sa_transport.calculate_energy(service_centers, locations)

print(f"Transportation network: {transport_energy:.3f} average distance to service centers")
print(f"Network has {len(road_network.nodes())} nodes and {len(road_network.edges())} edges")

Transportation network: 6.714 average distance to service centers
Network has 182 nodes and 181 edges




Multi-Scale Analysis

# Analyze clustering quality at different scales
scales = range(2, 6)
scale_energies = []

for k in scales:
    sa_scale = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
    centers_scale = sa_scale.run(initialization="kpp")
    energy_scale = sa_scale.calculate_energy(centers_scale, points)
    scale_energies.append(energy_scale)
    print(f"k={k}: energy={energy_scale:.3f}")

# Find the "elbow" - rapid decrease followed by diminishing returns
improvements = [scale_energies[i] - scale_energies[i+1] for i in range(len(scale_energies)-1)]
print(f"Improvements: {[f'{imp:.3f}' for imp in improvements]}")

k=2: energy=1.400
k=3: energy=1.250
k=4: energy=1.130
k=5: energy=0.780
Improvements: ['0.150', '0.120', '0.350']

title: Simulated Annealing
text:
Error Handling and Validation

Common Errors

# Demonstrate error handling
try:
    # Empty observations
    SimulatedAnnealing([], k=2)
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Empty observations error: {e}")

try:
    # Invalid k
    SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=0)
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Invalid k error: {e}")

try:
    # Invalid parameters
    SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=2, lambda_param=-1)
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Invalid lambda_param error: {e}")

def validate_clustering_setup(points, k, **params):

    if k > len(points) // 2:

    # Check if all points are from the same space
    if len(set(point.space for point in points)) > 1:
        raise ValueError("All points must be from the same metric space")

def benchmark_clustering(graph, n_points, k, description):
    """Benchmark clustering performance with detailed metrics."""

    # Run clustering

    centers = sa.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
    clustering_time = time.time() - start_time
    mem_after_clustering = profile_memory_usage()
    
    # Calculate energy

def optimize_graph_structure(graph, max_nodes=1000):
    """Optimize graph structure for better clustering performance."""
    
    original_nodes = len(graph.nodes())
    original_edges = len(graph.edges())
    
    print(f"Original graph: {original_nodes} nodes, {original_edges} edges")
    
    # Strategy 1: Work with largest connected component
    if not nx.is_connected(graph):
        largest_cc = max(nx.connected_components(graph), key=len)
        graph = graph.subgraph(largest_cc).copy()
        print(f"Extracted largest component: {len(graph.nodes())} nodes")
    
    # Strategy 2: Sample nodes if graph is too large
    if len(graph.nodes()) > max_nodes:
        # Sample nodes while preserving connectivity
        sampled_nodes = list(graph.nodes())[:max_nodes]
        graph = graph.subgraph(sampled_nodes).copy()
        
        # Ensure connectivity
        if not nx.is_connected(graph):
            largest_cc = max(nx.connected_components(graph), key=len)
            graph = graph.subgraph(largest_cc).copy()
        
        print(f"Sampled to {len(graph.nodes())} nodes")
    
    # Strategy 3: Precompute distances
    graph.precomputing()
    print("Distance precomputation completed")
    
    # Strategy 4: Validate edge weights for sampling efficiency
    edge_lengths = [d['length'] for u, v, d in graph.edges(data=True)]
    if edge_lengths:
        avg_length = np.mean(edge_lengths)
        std_length = np.std(edge_lengths)
        print(f"Edge lengths: μ={avg_length:.3f}, σ={std_length:.3f}")
        
        # Normalize extreme lengths if needed
        if std_length > 10 * avg_length:
            print("Warning: High variance in edge lengths may affect performance")
    
    final_nodes = len(graph.nodes())
    final_edges = len(graph.edges())
    reduction_factor = original_nodes / final_nodes if final_nodes > 0 else 1
    
    print(f"Final graph: {final_nodes} nodes, {final_edges} edges")
    print(f"Reduction factor: {reduction_factor:.2f}x")
    print()
    
    return graph

def get_fast_parameters(n_points, k, graph_density=0.1):
    """Get parameter settings optimized for speed."""
    
    base_params = {
        'lambda_param': 1.0,
        'beta': 1.0, 
        'step_size': 0.1
    }
    
    # Adjust for dataset size
    if n_points > 500:
        base_params['lambda_param'] = 2.0  # Higher exploration for large datasets
        base_params['step_size'] = 0.15    # Larger steps for faster convergence
    elif n_points < 100:
        base_params['beta'] = 2.0          # Faster convergence for small datasets
        base_params['step_size'] = 0.05    # Smaller steps for precision
    
    # Adjust for number of clusters
    if k > 5:
        base_params['lambda_param'] *= 1.5  # More exploration for many clusters
    
    # Adjust for graph density
    if graph_density > 0.2:  # Dense graph
        base_params['step_size'] *= 0.8    # Smaller steps in dense graphs
    elif graph_density < 0.05:  # Sparse graph
        base_params['step_size'] *= 1.3    # Larger steps in sparse graphs
    
    return base_params

def get_quality_parameters(n_points, k):
    """Get parameter settings optimized for clustering quality."""
    
    return {
        'lambda_param': 0.8,    # Moderate exploration
        'beta': 0.7,           # Slower convergence for better quality  
        'step_size': 0.05      # Smaller steps for precision
    }

def memory_efficient_clustering(graph, n_points, k, batch_size=100):
    """Perform clustering with memory optimization techniques."""
    
    print(f"=== Memory-Efficient Clustering ===")
    print(f"Processing {n_points} points in batches of {batch_size}")
    
    mem_start = profile_memory_usage()
    
    # Strategy 1: Batch processing of points
    all_points = []
    for batch_start in range(0, n_points, batch_size):
        batch_end = min(batch_start + batch_size, n_points)
        batch_points = graph.sample_points(batch_end - batch_start)
        all_points.extend(batch_points)
        
        if batch_start % (batch_size * 3) == 0:  # Report every 3 batches
            mem_current = profile_memory_usage()
            print(f"Batch {batch_start//batch_size + 1}: {mem_current:.1f} MB (+{mem_current - mem_start:.1f})")
    
    mem_after_sampling = profile_memory_usage()
    print(f"Sampling complete: {mem_after_sampling:.1f} MB")
    
    # Strategy 2: Use smaller robustification for memory efficiency
    sa = SimulatedAnnealing(all_points, k=k, step_size=0.15)  # Larger steps = faster = less memory over time
    centers = sa.run(robust_prop=0.05, initialization="kpp")  # Smaller robustification
    
    mem_after_clustering = profile_memory_usage()
    print(f"Clustering complete: {mem_after_clustering:.1f} MB")
    
    # Strategy 3: Incremental energy calculation
    total_energy = 0.0
    energy_batch_size = batch_size // 2
    
    for i in range(0, len(all_points), energy_batch_size):
        batch_points = all_points[i:i + energy_batch_size]
        batch_energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, batch_points)
        total_energy += batch_energy * len(batch_points)
        
    average_energy = total_energy / len(all_points)
    
    mem_final = profile_memory_usage()
    print(f"Final memory: {mem_final:.1f} MB")
    print(f"Peak memory usage: {max(mem_after_sampling, mem_after_clustering, mem_final):.1f} MB")
    print(f"Average energy: {average_energy:.3f}")
    
    return centers, average_energy

# Test memory-efficient approach
test_graph_large = generate_sbm(sizes=[80, 80, 60], p=[[0.7, 0.1, 0.1], [0.1, 0.7, 0.1], [0.1, 0.1, 0.7]])

def parallel_initialization_comparison(points, k, n_trials=5):
    """Compare different initialization strategies for consistency and speed."""
    
    print(f"=== Initialization Strategy Comparison ===")
    print(f"Testing with {len(points)} points, k={k}, {n_trials} trials each")
    
    strategies = ['random', 'kpp']
    results = {}
    
    for strategy in strategies:
        energies = []
        times = []
        
        print(f"\nTesting {strategy} initialization:")
        
        for trial in range(n_trials):
            start_time = time.time()
            sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
            centers = sa.run(initialization=strategy, robust_prop=0.1)
            end_time = time.time()
            
            energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
            runtime = end_time - start_time
            
            energies.append(energy)
            times.append(runtime)
            
            if trial < 3:  # Show first few trials
                print(f"  Trial {trial + 1}: {runtime:.3f}s, energy={energy:.3f}")
        
        # Statistics
        mean_energy = np.mean(energies)
        std_energy = np.std(energies)
        mean_time = np.mean(times)
        std_time = np.std(times)
        
        results[strategy] = {
            'mean_energy': mean_energy,
            'std_energy': std_energy,
            'mean_time': mean_time,
            'std_time': std_time,
            'consistency': std_energy / mean_energy  # Lower is more consistent
        }
        
        print(f"  Average: {mean_time:.3f}±{std_time:.3f}s, energy={mean_energy:.3f}±{std_energy:.3f}")
        print(f"  Consistency score: {results[strategy]['consistency']:.4f}")
    
    # Recommendation
    if results['kpp']['consistency'] < results['random']['consistency']:
        print(f"\nRecommendation: Use k-means++ for better consistency")
    else:
        print(f"\nRecommendation: Random initialization is sufficient")
    
    if results['kpp']['mean_time'] > results['random']['mean_time'] * 1.5:
        print(f"Note: k-means++ is {results['kpp']['mean_time'] / results['random']['mean_time']:.1f}x slower")
    
    return results

def compare_algorithm_versions(points, k):
    """Compare algorithm versions for different scenarios."""
    
    print(f"=== Algorithm Version Comparison ===")
    print(f"Dataset: {len(points)} points, k={k}")
    
    versions = ['v1', 'v2']
    version_results = {}
    
    for version in versions:
        print(f"\nTesting algorithm {version}:")
        
        # Multiple runs for stability
        energies = []
        times = []
        
        for run in range(3):
            start_time = time.time()
            sa = SimulatedAnnealing(points, k=k)
            centers = sa.run(
                algorithm_version=version,
                robust_prop=0.1,
                initialization="kpp"
            )
            end_time = time.time()
            
            energy = sa.calculate_energy(centers, points)
            runtime = end_time - start_time
            
            energies.append(energy)
            times.append(runtime)
            
            print(f"  Run {run + 1}: {runtime:.3f}s, energy={energy:.3f}")
        
        mean_energy = np.mean(energies)
        mean_time = np.mean(times)
        
        version_results[version] = {
            'mean_energy': mean_energy,
            'mean_time': mean_time,
            'energies': energies,
            'times': times
        }
        
        print(f"  Average: {mean_time:.3f}s, energy={mean_energy:.3f}")
    
    # Analysis and recommendation
    v1_result = version_results['v1']
    v2_result = version_results['v2']
    
    speed_ratio = v1_result['mean_time'] / v2_result['mean_time']
    energy_ratio = v1_result['mean_energy'] / v2_result['mean_energy']
    
    print(f"\n=== Comparison Analysis ===")
    print(f"Speed: v1 vs v2 = {speed_ratio:.2f} (lower is faster)")
    print(f"Energy: v1 vs v2 = {energy_ratio:.3f} (lower is better)")
    
    if speed_ratio < 0.9:
        print("Recommendation: Use v1 (interleaved) for better speed")
    elif speed_ratio > 1.1:
        print("Recommendation: Use v2 (sequential) for better speed")
    else:
        print("Recommendation: Both versions have similar performance")
    
    if abs(energy_ratio - 1.0) > 0.05:
        better_quality = "v1" if energy_ratio < 1.0 else "v2"
        print(f"Note: {better_quality} provides better clustering quality")
    
    return version_results

def create_performance_monitor():
    """Create a simple performance monitoring utility."""
    
    class PerformanceMonitor:
        def __init__(self):
            self.start_time = None
            self.start_memory = None
            self.checkpoints = []
        
        def start(self):
            self.start_time = time.time()
            self.start_memory = profile_memory_usage()
            self.checkpoints = []
            print(f"Performance monitoring started (Memory: {self.start_memory:.1f} MB)")
        
        def checkpoint(self, name):
            current_time = time.time()
            current_memory = profile_memory_usage()
            
            elapsed = current_time - self.start_time
            memory_delta = current_memory - self.start_memory
            
            self.checkpoints.append({
                'name': name,
                'elapsed': elapsed,
                'memory': current_memory,
                'memory_delta': memory_delta
            })
            
            print(f"Checkpoint '{name}': {elapsed:.3f}s, {current_memory:.1f} MB (+{memory_delta:+.1f})")
        
        def finish(self):
            if self.checkpoints:
                total_time = self.checkpoints[-1]['elapsed']
                peak_memory = max(cp['memory'] for cp in self.checkpoints)
                print(f"Monitoring complete: {total_time:.3f}s total, {peak_memory:.1f} MB peak")
            return self.checkpoints
    
    return PerformanceMonitor()

# Example usage of performance monitor

monitor.start()

test_points_monitor = test_graph_monitor.sample_points(150)

monitor.checkpoint("SA initialized")

centers_monitor = sa_monitor.run(initialization="kpp", robust_prop=0.1)
monitor.checkpoint("Clustering complete")

energy_monitor = sa_monitor.calculate_energy(centers_monitor, test_points_monitor)
monitor.checkpoint("Energy calculated")

checkpoints = monitor.finish()

# Bottleneck analysis
print(f"\nBottleneck analysis:")
for i in range(1, len(checkpoints)):
    step_time = checkpoints[i]['elapsed'] - checkpoints[i-1]['elapsed']
    step_memory = checkpoints[i]['memory_delta'] - checkpoints[i-1]['memory_delta']
    print(f"{checkpoints[i-1]['name']} → {checkpoints[i]['name']}: {step_time:.3f}s, {step_memory:+.1f} MB")
